statement: Do not assume a reference-back summary is sufficient in excerpt-library conditions merely because it works in continuous book reading.
what_it_prevents: False sufficiency judgments that leave the owner conceptually under-supported.

statement: Do not force the owner to manhunt for the earlier material an author is explicitly relying on.
what_it_prevents: Manual backtracking burden that the library should absorb.

statement: Do not discard the author’s intended flow and reader assumptions when translating a continuous-reading book into excerpts.
what_it_prevents: Author-flow blindness and hidden context loss.

statement: Do not treat all reference-back notes as equivalent; orientational reminders and concept-building recalls need different handling.
Do not reduce the unit to a pure quote when real context-restoration support is needed for understanding.
what_it_prevents: A brittle quote-only system that fails under zero-precontext use.

statement: Do not let support-layer expansion displace the original source text from the center of the unit.
what_it_prevents: Loss of source centrality and drift into overproduced synthetic units.

statement: Do not lose passage-level visibility when an excerpt relies on a larger earlier passage or chapter segment.
what_it_prevents: Hidden context loss and unresolved reference-back dependency.

statement: Do not assume excerpt-to-excerpt links alone are always enough when the referred material is broader than a neat excerpt cluster.
what_it_prevents: Chaotic chains of links that still fail to restore the needed context.

statement: Do not design context support as if the owner always remembers prior discussion or has another book/tab open.
what_it_prevents: A library that still leans on owner reconstruction instead of full guidance.

statement: Do not let the teaching-unit expansion become uncontrolled just because the case reveals real pressure to go beyond pure excerpts.
what_it_prevents: Chaotic overexpansion and loss of protocol discipline.
